wrongChoiceReasons.A: Past simple usually needs a specific finished time like 'last year', but 'three times' is about life experience up to now.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Have gone to' means the person is still there now, but you are talking about past trips that are completed.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Was' doesn't work with 'to Spain' — we would need 'was in Spain' instead.

explanation: Fantastic! 'Have been' is used here because we're talking about life experiences at unspecified times in the past.
explanationSteps: The phrase 'three times' tells us we're talking about experiences up to now, not one specific past moment.
We use 'have/has been to' for places we visited and returned from.
Since these trips are finished and you're not in Spain now, 'have been' fits perfectly!
hints: Which tense do we use for life experiences without a specific time?
Remember: 'been to' means visited and came back. 'Gone to' means still there!
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: I ______ to Spain three times with my class. The food there is amazing!

explanation: Excellent! The past perfect helps us show which past action happened first.
explanationSteps: Two things happened in the past: arriving at school, and the competition starting.
The competition started BEFORE the arrival. For the earlier past action, we use past perfect: 'had + past participle'.
'Had started' clearly shows the competition was already in progress when you got there.
hints: Which action happened first: arriving at school or the competition starting?
For the earlier of two past actions, use 'had + past participle'.
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: When we arrived at school, the project competition ______ already ______. (start)

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Will' is great for spontaneous decisions, but here we have clear evidence in the sky right now!
wrongChoiceReasons.C: We use present simple for schedules and routines, not for predictions based on what we see.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Present continuous describes what is happening at this exact moment, but it isn't raining yet.

explanation: Super observation! When we have visible evidence right now, 'going to' is the perfect choice.
explanationSteps: Dark clouds are clear evidence we can see in the present moment.
Use 'be going to' when there is present evidence that something will happen.
'Will rain' is more for general predictions or decisions made at the moment of speaking.
hints: Do we have evidence we can see right now?
When we notice signs in the present, 'going to' is usually the best friend!
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: Look at those dark clouds! I think it ______ soon.

explanation: Good eye! We need the present perfect continuous to show an activity that started in the past and is still happening.
explanationSteps: 'For two hours' tells us the action started in the past and continues up to now.
The present perfect continuous uses 'have/has been + verb-ing'.
'Has been played' is passive voice, but your brother is the one doing the action!
hints: Which tense shows an action that started in the past and is still continuing?
Look at the structure: 'has been + ...-ing' or 'has been + past participle'?
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: My brother has been played basketball for two hours. He must be tired!

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Past simple needs a specific time reference like 'last summer', but 'ever' asks about your whole life.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present continuous is for actions happening right now, not life experiences.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Present simple is for habits, but riding a camel is an experience, not a daily routine.

explanation: Perfect! 'Ever' is a wonderful signal that we need the present perfect to ask about life experiences.
explanationSteps: 'Ever' is a time word that pairs beautifully with the present perfect.
Questions about life experiences use 'Have/Has + subject + ever + past participle?'
'Ridden' is the past participle of 'ride' — it might look unusual, but it's correct!
hints: Which word in the sentence is a clue for life experiences?
Present perfect questions often start with 'Have you ever...'
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: ______ you ever ______ a camel in the desert?

explanation: Great work! 'By the time' often introduces a later past action, so the main verb needs past perfect.
explanationSteps: The teacher collecting homework is a past action. Finishing the essay happened BEFORE that.
For the earlier past action, use 'had + past participle'.
'By the time' is a helpful phrase that often signals the past perfect is coming!
hints: Which happened first: finishing the essay or the teacher collecting it?
Use 'had + past participle' for the action that was completed before another past action.
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: By the time the teacher collected the homework, I ______ ______ my essay. (finish)

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Will attend' sounds like a decision made right now, but a meeting is usually a fixed plan.
wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Going to' works for intentions, but present continuous is the go-to choice for diary arrangements.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present simple is for regular habits, not a specific meeting tomorrow.

explanation: Exactly! When we have a definite future plan or arrangement, the present continuous works beautifully.
explanationSteps: The sentence includes a specific future time: 'tomorrow'.
When we talk about fixed arrangements or plans in our diary, we can use the present continuous.
This is different from 'will' (decisions) and 'going to' (intentions with evidence).
hints: Is a school meeting usually a fixed plan or a sudden decision?
For definite future arrangements already scheduled, present continuous is your best choice!
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: I can't come to the beach cleanup tomorrow because I ______ a school project meeting.

originalSentence: I last played tennis two weeks ago.
answerKey: I haven't played tennis for two weeks.
acceptableAnswers: I have not played tennis for two weeks.
explanation: Excellent transformation! You connected the past action to the present moment perfectly.
explanationSteps: 'I last played... ago' tells us the action stopped in the past and continues up to now.
The present perfect negative 'haven't played' shows no tennis during this period.
We use 'for + period of time' to say how long the situation has continued.
hints: How can you make 'played' into a present perfect form?
Use 'for' with the time period 'two weeks'.
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: Rewrite the sentence using the present perfect. Start with 'I haven't...'

wrongChoiceReasons.B: This reverses the tenses! 'Last summer' needs past simple, and 'before that' needs present perfect.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present simple 'is' doesn't work with 'last summer', which is finished past time.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Past perfect 'had been' is too early here — 'last summer' is a specific past time that needs past simple.

explanation: Beautiful! You matched each time expression with its perfect tense partner.
explanationSteps: 'Last summer' is a specific, finished time in the past, so we use past simple: 'was'.
'Many times before that' tells us about experiences up to now (including last summer), so present perfect 'has been' is ideal.
Keeping these two tenses separate is a great skill to have!
hints: Which tense do we use with 'last summer' — a finished time?
Which tense do we use for experiences before now without a specific time?
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: She ______ in London last summer, but she ______ there many times before that too.

explanation: Not quite! While they are similar, 'will' and 'going to' have slightly different jobs.
explanationSteps: 'Will' can express a spontaneous decision or a general prediction.
'Going to' is used when there is present evidence or a prior plan.
In many cases they are interchangeable, but they don't ALWAYS mean exactly the same thing — good catch!
hints: Think about a decision made at the moment of speaking. Which form fits better?
Does 'going to' suggest a plan or evidence?
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.
prompt: 'We will fly to Paris next month' and 'We are going to fly to Paris next month' always mean exactly the same thing.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Would hold' is for second conditional (imaginary situations), but this is a real possibility.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present simple in the main clause doesn't work for future results of if-clauses.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Past simple is used in the if-clause of second conditionals, not the main clause.

explanation: Perfect! This is a first conditional — a real possibility in the future with a likely result.
explanationSteps: The if-clause uses present simple: 'it rains'.
For first conditional main clauses, use 'will + infinitive' for the likely result.
First conditional is all about real, possible futures — like a backup plan for your sports day!
hints: Is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation?
First conditional: if + present simple, ... will + infinitive.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: If it rains this weekend, we ______ our school sports day indoors.

explanation: Wonderful! You've entered the world of imagination with the second conditional.
explanationSteps: 'If I were a professional athlete' describes an imaginary situation, not reality.
In second conditional, we use past simple in the if-clause and 'would + infinitive' in the main clause.
Even for 'I', we often use 'were' instead of 'was' in formal conditional sentences — both are acceptable!
hints: Is becoming a professional athlete a certain plan or an imaginary dream?
Second conditional structure: if + past simple, ... would + infinitive.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: If I ______ a professional athlete, I ______ travel around the world for competitions. (be)

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Would win' is for imaginary situations, but winning a competition you're in is a real possibility.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Would have won' is third conditional — for past situations that didn't happen. Too complex for this real future possibility!
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Future perfect 'will have won' talks about completion before a future time, but here we need a simple result.

explanation: Exactly right! This first conditional describes a real future possibility and its happy result.
explanationSteps: Your team entering a competition is a real situation with a possible future outcome.
First conditional structure: if + present simple, ... will + base verb.
The prize — a trip to the nature reserve — is the likely result of winning!
hints: Is this about a real future event or an imaginary one?
First conditional result clause uses 'will + base verb'.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: If my team wins the science competition, we ______ a trip to the nature reserve.

explanation: Great catch! In conditional sentences, the if-clause and main clause need to match in type.
explanationSteps: The main clause uses 'would pass', which tells us this is a second conditional (imaginary situation).
Second conditional if-clauses use past simple, not 'will'.
So 'studied' matches perfectly with 'would pass' to create an imaginary scenario.
hints: Does 'would pass' suggest a real possibility or an imaginary situation?
In second conditionals, the if-clause uses past simple, not 'will'.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: If I will study harder, I would pass my exams with better grades.

originalSentence: I don't know how to ski, so I can't join the winter trip.
answerKey: If I knew how to ski, I could join the winter trip.
acceptableAnswers: If I knew how to ski, I would be able to join the winter trip.
explanation: Brilliant transformation! You turned a real limitation into an imaginary possibility.
explanationSteps: The original sentence describes a real situation: not knowing how to ski.
Second conditional imagines a different reality: 'If I knew...'
The result uses 'could' (or 'would be able to') to show the imaginary ability.
hints: Change the negative real situation into an imaginary positive one.
Use past simple in the if-clause and 'could' for ability in the main clause.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: Rewrite this sentence as a second conditional. Begin with 'If...'

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Would lose' is for imaginary or unlikely situations, but environmental damage is a real future possibility.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Past simple 'lost' doesn't fit a future consequence of a present action.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Third conditional 'would have lost' refers to the past, but this is about our future choices.

explanation: Well done! This first conditional reminds us that real actions have real future consequences.
explanationSteps: The sentence talks about a real future consequence of our current choices.
First conditional uses 'if + present simple, ... will + infinitive'.
It shows that protecting the oceans matters for the future!
hints: Is this about an imaginary world or a real future we can still change?
First conditional result: 'will + infinitive'.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: If we don't protect the oceans, many sea animals ______ their homes.

explanation: Almost, but not quite! 'When' and 'if' are close friends, but they have different meanings.
explanationSteps: 'If' means the situation is possible but not certain.
'When' means the situation is certain to happen at some point.
So 'When I see him...' (certain) and 'If I see him...' (possible) have slightly different meanings!
hints: Does 'if' mean something will definitely happen?
Think about certainty vs. possibility.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: In a first conditional sentence, we can use 'when' instead of 'if' without changing the meaning at all.

explanation: Excellent! You've used the second conditional to imagine a positive change at your school.
explanationSteps: A new recycling program is an idea, not yet a reality — so we use the imaginary second conditional.
If-clause: past simple 'started'.
Main clause: 'would + infinitive' for the imagined result.
hints: Is the recycling program already happening, or is it just an idea?
Second conditional: if + past simple, ... would + infinitive.
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.
prompt: If my school ______ a recycling program, we ______ reduce a lot of waste. (start)

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Are collecting' is active voice, but bottles don't collect themselves — people collect them!
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Active voice 'collect' doesn't work because the subject (bottles) receives the action.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Past passive 'were collected' doesn't match 'every day', which is a present time expression.

explanation: Spot on! The bottles receive the action, so we need the present passive voice.
explanationSteps: The subject 'plastic bottles' receives the action — they are collected by someone.
Present passive uses 'am/is/are + past participle'.
'Every day' tells us this is a regular present activity, so 'are collected' is perfect.
hints: Do the bottles do the collecting, or are they collected by someone?
Present passive structure: am/is/are + past participle.
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: Plastic bottles ______ at the new recycling center every day.

explanation: Perfect! The projects received the action, and 'yesterday' places us in the past.
explanationSteps: The projects didn't evaluate themselves — the judges did it, so we need passive voice.
'Yesterday' tells us to use past passive: 'was/were + past participle'.
Since 'projects' is plural, we use 'were evaluated'.
hints: Did the projects do the evaluating?
Past passive for plural subjects: 'were + past participle'.
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: The science fair projects ______ ______ by the judges yesterday. (evaluate)

originalSentence: Tourists discovered the ancient temple in 1987.
answerKey: The ancient temple was discovered by tourists in 1987.
acceptableAnswers: The ancient temple was discovered in 1987 by tourists.
explanation: Wonderful! You shifted the focus from the tourists to the temple — that's the magic of passive voice.
explanationSteps: The object of the active sentence ('the ancient temple') becomes the subject of the passive sentence.
Past passive structure: 'was/were + past participle'. 'Was discovered' fits perfectly.
We can include 'by tourists' if we want to mention who did it, or we can leave it out!
hints: Make 'the ancient temple' the new subject.
Use 'was + past participle' because the temple is singular and the discovery is in the past.
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: Change this sentence to the passive voice. Begin with 'The ancient temple...'

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Present passive doesn't fit because 'after the match' refers to a specific finished past event.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Active voice 'presents' would mean the trophy gives itself, which doesn't make sense!
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Active voice 'has presented' also suggests the trophy did the presenting — the trophy receives the action.

explanation: Excellent! The trophy receives the action, and the match is over, so past passive is the winner.
explanationSteps: The trophy is given to the team, so it receives the action (passive voice needed).
'After the match' tells us this happened at a specific time in the past.
Past passive: 'was/were + past participle'. 'Was presented' fits perfectly!
hints: Does the trophy do the presenting, or is it presented?
Is the match happening now or is it finished?
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: The championship trophy ______ to the winning team by the coach after the match.

explanation: Great catch! We need the passive form to show that volunteers do the cleaning.
explanationSteps: The park doesn't clean itself — volunteers clean it, so we need passive voice.
'Is clean' is an adjective describing the park.
To show the action, we need 'is cleaned' (passive verb) or 'gets cleaned'.
hints: Is 'clean' here describing a state or an action?
Passive voice needs a past participle: 'is + cleaned'.
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: The local park is clean every Saturday by volunteers.

explanation: Not necessarily! The agent is often left out, especially when it's obvious or unimportant.
explanationSteps: Passive voice focuses on the action or the receiver, not the doer.
We use 'by + agent' only when the doer is important or surprising.
For example: 'The homework was finished' — we don't need to say 'by me' if everyone already knows!
hints: Think about sentences like 'The window was broken.' Do we always need to say by whom?
Passive voice is useful when the doer is unknown, obvious, or unimportant.
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: In passive sentences, we must always include the agent (the person or thing doing the action).

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Active voice suggests the station turns itself, but people will transform it.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present simple 'turns' is for habits and facts, not future plans for transformation.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Conditional 'would turn' needs an if-clause, and this is a real future plan.

explanation: Perfect! The station will receive the transformation, so future passive is exactly what we need.
explanationSteps: The train station is the receiver of the action, not the doer.
'Next year' tells us we're looking at the future.
Future passive uses 'will be + past participle': 'will be turned'.
hints: Will the station transform itself, or will someone transform it?
Future passive structure: 'will be + past participle'.
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: The old train station ______ ______ into a museum next year.

explanation: Excellent! The match received the action of canceling due to the weather.
explanationSteps: The match didn't cancel itself — the organizers did, so passive voice is needed.
The cancellation happened in the past, so we use past passive.
In British English: 'cancelled'. In American English: 'canceled'. Both are welcome here!
hints: Did the match cancel itself?
Past passive: 'was/were + past participle' for a singular subject.
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.
prompt: The football match ______ ______ because of the heavy rain. (cancel)

wrongChoiceReasons.B: While 'choir' contains many people, it acts as one unit in this sentence.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Were' is past tense, but 'tomorrow night' is in the future.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Present perfect 'have been' doesn't work with a specific future time.

explanation: Perfect! When a collective noun acts as a single unit, a singular verb is the natural choice.
explanationSteps: 'The school choir' can be seen as one unit performing together.
When emphasizing the group acting together, use a singular verb: 'is singing'.
'Tomorrow night' tells us this is a future arrangement, so present continuous with 'is' fits perfectly.
hints: Is the choir acting as one unit or as separate individuals here?
Collective nouns often take singular verbs when the group acts together.
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.
prompt: The school choir ______ singing at the concert tomorrow night.

explanation: Excellent! The real subject is 'the book' (singular), even though other words come between it and the verb.
explanationSteps: Find the main subject by removing the prepositional phrases: 'about endangered animals' and 'on these shelves'.
The real subject is 'the book', which is singular.
So the verb needs the '-s' ending: 'needs'.
hints: Cross out the prepositional phrases to find the true subject.
Is the true subject singular or plural?
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.
prompt: The book about endangered animals on these shelves need to be returned to the library.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: In American English, 'group' as a single unit takes a singular verb. Even in British English, when emphasizing the unit, 'has' is preferred.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present simple 'visit' doesn't work with 'so far this summer', which needs present perfect.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Present continuous 'are visiting' doesn't fit with 'so far', which covers a period up to now.

explanation: Perfect! When we think of a group as a single unit, a singular verb is the way to go.
explanationSteps: 'Our travel group' can be seen as one single unit moving together.
When emphasizing the group as a unit, use a singular verb: 'has visited'.
'So far this summer' is a present perfect time expression, which matches perfectly.
hints: Is the group traveling as one unit, or are we focusing on each person separately?
Present perfect with 'so far': has/have + past participle.
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.
prompt: Our travel group ______ ten different countries so far this summer.

explanation: Great job! With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the noun closest to it.
explanationSteps: In 'neither...nor' constructions, look at the noun nearest to the verb.
Here, 'the students' is plural and closest to the verb gap.
So we use the plural verb 'are'. If it were 'neither the students nor the teacher', we'd use 'is'!
hints: With 'neither...nor', which noun is closer to the verb?
The verb agrees with the nearest subject.
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.
prompt: Neither the teacher nor the students ______ worried about the upcoming exam.

explanation: Not quite! 'A number of' actually means 'many', and it takes a plural verb.
explanationSteps: 'A number of students' means 'many students'.
Because it refers to many individual people, we use a plural verb: 'A number of students are...'
This is different from 'the number of students', which IS singular: 'The number of students is growing.'
hints: Does 'a number of' mean one thing or many things?
Think about whether we're talking about many individuals or a single figure.
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.
prompt: The phrase 'a number of students' always takes a singular verb because 'number' is singular.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: It might seem plural because of 'trails', but the real subject is 'map', which is singular.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Past tense 'were' doesn't fit if we're describing a current fact about the visitor center.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Present perfect 'have been' suggests a connection to now, but this is a simple current fact.

explanation: Excellent! You looked past the extra words to find the true subject.
explanationSteps: Remove the prepositional phrase 'of the hiking trails' to find the real subject.
The subject is 'the map', which is singular.
So we need the singular verb 'is'.
hints: Cross out 'of the hiking trails' to find the real subject.
Is the true subject singular or plural?
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.
prompt: The map of the hiking trails ______ available at the visitor center.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: We say 'arrive at' for specific points or smaller places, not 'arrive to'.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Arrive in' is used for large cities or countries, not specific buildings like airports.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Arrive on' is used for surfaces like islands or platforms, not airports.

explanation: Perfect! 'Arrive at' is the standard partner for specific places like airports, stations, and schools.
explanationSteps: We use 'arrive at' for specific points and smaller locations (airport, station, hotel).
We use 'arrive in' for larger areas (cities, countries, continents).
Since an airport is a specific point, 'at' is the perfect preposition!
hints: Is an airport a large city or a specific point?
We 'arrive at' specific places and 'arrive in' large areas.
Think about the physical picture — where is the object in relation to the other one?
prompt: We arrived ______ the airport just in time for our flight to Tokyo.

explanation: Excellent! You've matched the adjective and noun with their preposition partners.
explanationSteps: 'Proud' pairs with 'of' to show the reason for pride.
For the second gap, 'on' or 'for' can introduce the topic of a project.
'Proud of' is a fixed collocation — they always go together!
hints: Which preposition follows 'proud'?
Which preposition introduces the topic of a project?
Think about the physical picture — where is the object in relation to the other one?
prompt: The teacher is very proud ______ the students' work ______ the recycling project.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'At' is used for specific times like 'at 5 o'clock' or 'at night', not months.
wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'On' is for specific days and dates like 'on Monday' or 'on July 4th', not whole months.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'By July' means before or no later than July, which doesn't fit the meaning of camping during July.

explanation: Great! Months always pair with the preposition 'in'.
explanationSteps: We use 'in' for months, seasons, years, and longer periods.
July is a month, so 'in July' is the natural choice.
Remember: in July, on Monday, at 5 o'clock!
hints: Which preposition do we use with months?
Think: in months, on days, at times.
Think about the physical picture — where is the object in relation to the other one?
prompt: We always go camping ______ July because the weather is perfect.

explanation: Great catch! 'Keen' has a special preposition partner when we talk about liking something.
explanationSteps: The adjective 'keen' pairs with 'on' when we want to say we really like or want to do something.
'Keen in' is not a standard English collocation.
'Keen on joining' or 'keen to join' are both excellent options!
hints: Which preposition usually follows 'keen' when talking about interests?
'Keen on' is a standard collocation in English.
Think about the physical picture — where is the object in relation to the other one?
prompt: My little brother is really keen in joining the school basketball team.

explanation: Not quite! 'Depend' always pairs with 'on' — they are inseparable friends.
explanationSteps: 'Depend on' is the correct collocation in standard English.
'Depend of' is a common influence from some other languages, but it's not standard English.
Other correct options: 'depend upon' (more formal) or 'it depends' (standalone).
hints: Think about the phrase 'It depends ___ the weather.' Which preposition fits?
'Depend' and 'on' are a fixed pair in English.
Think about the physical picture — where is the object in relation to the other one?
prompt: We can say 'depend of' in English when we want to say that something relies on something else.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Turn out' can work with lights in some contexts, but 'turn off' is the most common and natural phrasal verb.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Turn down' means to reduce volume or heat, not to stop lights completely.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Turn away' means to refuse entry or look in another direction, not to operate a switch.

explanation: Perfect! 'Turn off' is the everyday phrasal verb we use for lights, TVs, and electronic devices.
explanationSteps: 'Turn off' means to stop a machine, light, or device from working.
It's the standard phrasal verb for light switches.
Other options: 'turn on' (start), 'turn up' (increase), 'turn down' (decrease).
hints: Which phrasal verb means to stop a light from shining?
Think about the opposite of 'turn on'.
Think about the physical picture — where is the object in relation to the other one?
prompt: Please turn ______ the lights when you leave the classroom to save energy.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Must' means we are very certain — almost 100%. But 'I'm not sure' tells us the opposite!
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Should' gives advice or expectation, but here we're talking about possibility, not what ought to happen.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Can' refers to ability or general possibility, but for a specific future possibility with uncertainty, 'might' is better.

explanation: Excellent! 'Might' shows that rain is possible but not certain — exactly what we need here.
explanationSteps: 'I'm not sure' tells us we need a modal that shows uncertainty.
'Might' is perfect for a possibility that could happen but probably won't.
It's a gentle, careful way to talk about the future weather!
hints: Are we certain it will rain, or is it just a possibility?
For uncertain possibilities, 'might' or 'may' are your best choices.
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.
prompt: I'm not sure, but it ______ rain later, so take an umbrella just in case.

explanation: Perfect! 'Should' is the friendly modal we use to give helpful advice.
explanationSteps: The sentence gives good advice about staying healthy during sports.
'Should' and 'ought to' both give advice or recommendations.
'Should drink' is the most natural choice for this friendly piece of advice.
hints: Which modal do we use to give helpful advice?
'Should' + base verb is a great way to recommend something.
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.
prompt: You ______ ______ more water during sports practice to stay healthy.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Can have rained' is not used for deductions about the past in standard English; we use 'could have' or 'must have'.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Should have rained' means we expected rain but it didn't necessarily happen — not a deduction from evidence.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'May have rained' is possible, but 'must have' is stronger and better for logical deductions from clear evidence.

explanation: Brilliant deduction! When we have strong evidence and we're almost sure, 'must have' is the perfect modal.
explanationSteps: Wet ground is strong evidence that rain happened.
'Must have + past participle' is how we make logical deductions about the past.
It means we are very confident — almost certain — based on what we see now.
hints: How certain are we, based on the wet ground?
For strong logical deductions about the past, use 'must have + past participle'.
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.
prompt: The ground is wet outside. It ______ have rained during the night.

explanation: Great catch! After 'must', we always use the base form of the verb without 'to'.
explanationSteps: 'Must' is a modal verb, and modal verbs are followed directly by the base verb.
So we say 'must finish', not 'must to finish'.
'Have to' is different — it DOES use 'to': 'have to finish'.
hints: Modal verbs are special — do they need 'to' before the next verb?
After 'must', use the base form directly: 'must + finish'.
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.
prompt: You must to finish your homework before the sports match starts.

originalSentence: It is possible that the train will not arrive on time.
answerKey: The train might not arrive on time.
acceptableAnswers: The train may not arrive on time.
explanation: Excellent transformation! You've turned a longer possibility phrase into a smooth modal sentence.
explanationSteps: 'It is possible that... will not' can be replaced by 'might not' for a cleaner sentence.
'Might not' shows that something is possibly not going to happen.
The subject 'the train' moves to the front, making the sentence more direct and natural.
hints: Which subject should start the new sentence?
Replace 'It is possible that... will not' with 'might not' before the main verb.
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.
prompt: Rewrite this sentence using 'might not'. Keep the same meaning about possibility.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Who' is for people, not places like hotels.
wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Which' could work if there were a preposition ('in which'), but alone it doesn't fit after 'the hotel'.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Whose' shows possession, but we need a place word here.

explanation: Perfect! 'Where' is the relative adverb that replaces 'in which' for places.
explanationSteps: The hotel is a place, so we need a relative word for places.
'Where' means 'in/at which' and is used for places.
You can also say 'in which we stayed', but 'where' is much more natural!
hints: Is the hotel a person, a thing, or a place?
For places, 'where' is often the most natural relative word.
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.
prompt: This is the hotel ______ we stayed during our school trip to London.

explanation: Excellent! For people in defining relative clauses, 'who' is the natural choice.
explanationSteps: The antecedent is 'the athlete', which is a person.
In defining relative clauses for people, we use 'who' (or 'that').
The clause 'who won the gold medal' defines WHICH athlete we mean.
hints: Are we talking about a person or a thing?
For people in defining relative clauses, 'who' works perfectly.
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.
prompt: The athlete ______ won the gold medal trains at our local sports center.

explanation: Great catch! 'Who' is reserved for people, not objects like bikes.
explanationSteps: The antecedent is 'the bike', which is a thing, not a person.
For things in defining relative clauses, we use 'that' or 'which'.
'Who' is only for people and sometimes pets with names!
hints: Is a bike a person?
For things, 'that' or 'which' are the right choices.
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.
prompt: The bike who I bought last week has a flat tire.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Who' would be the subject of the clause, but here we need to show possession of the team.
wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Which' is for things, not people, and it doesn't show possession.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Whom' is the object form of 'who', used for people, but it doesn't show possession either.

explanation: Perfect! 'Whose' shows that the team belongs to the coach — it's the possessive relative pronoun.
explanationSteps: We need to show that the team belongs to the coach.
'Whose' is the possessive form for both people and things in relative clauses.
It works like 'his', 'her', or 'its' but in a relative clause.
hints: Does the clause need to show who the team belongs to?
For possession in relative clauses, 'whose' is the key.
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.
prompt: The coach ______ team won the championship was interviewed on TV.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Many' is for countable nouns like 'bottles', but 'milk' is uncountable.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Few' is for countable nouns and means almost none. It doesn't work with uncountable 'milk'.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'A few' means some, and it's also for countable nouns. Plus, the sentence says there isn't any left!

explanation: Excellent! 'Much' is the quantifier we use with uncountable nouns in negative sentences.
explanationSteps: 'Milk' is an uncountable noun — you can't count it as one milk, two milk.
In negative sentences with uncountable nouns, 'much' is the standard quantifier.
'Many', 'few', and 'a few' are all for countable nouns like bottles or cartons.
hints: Is 'milk' countable or uncountable?
For uncountable nouns in negative sentences, which quantifier do we use?
Try saying the sentence out loud with each option — your ear often knows the right article.
prompt: There isn't ______ milk left in the fridge. Could you buy some on your way home?

explanation: Perfect! 'Astronaut' starts with a vowel sound, so it needs 'an'.
explanationSteps: We use 'an' before words that start with a vowel sound (a, e, i, o, u).
'Astronaut' begins with the vowel sound 'a'.
It's the sound that matters, not just the letter — for example, 'a university' (yoo sound) but 'an hour' (silent h).
hints: What sound does 'astronaut' start with — a vowel or a consonant?
Before vowel sounds, we use 'an' instead of 'a'.
Try saying the sentence out loud with each option — your ear often knows the right article.
prompt: My brother wants to be ______ astronaut when he grows up because he loves space.

explanation: Exactly right! 'Some' isn't just for positive sentences — it's friendly in offers and requests too.
explanationSteps: 'Some' is commonly used in offers: 'Would you like some water?'
It's also used in requests when we expect a positive answer: 'Could I have some water, please?'
This is a lovely exception to the 'some for positive, any for questions' rule!
hints: Think about offering a friend a snack. Would you say 'Would you like any cookies?' or 'Would you like some cookies?'
Offers and requests with positive expectations often use 'some'.
Try saying the sentence out loud with each option — your ear often knows the right article.
prompt: We can use 'some' in questions when we expect the answer to be yes or when we are offering something.

explanation: Excellent! You spotted that the action started in the past and is still happening now.
explanationSteps: 'For twenty minutes' tells us the waiting started in the past and continues up to now.
The present perfect continuous uses 'have/has been + verb-ing'.
Since 'we' is plural, we use 'have been waiting'.
hints: Which tense do we use for an action that started in the past and is still going on?
Remember: 'have been + ...-ing' for actions continuing up to now.
The subject is plural, so use 'have' not 'has'.
prompt: We ______ ______ ______ for the school bus for twenty minutes. I hope it comes soon!

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Present simple 'breaks' is for habits and routines, but yesterday is a finished past time.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present perfect needs a connection to now, but 'yesterday' is a specific finished time.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Past continuous 'was breaking' suggests an ongoing action, but breaking a leg is usually a single event.

explanation: Great work! 'Yesterday' is a clear signal that we need the past simple tense.
explanationSteps: 'Yesterday' tells us the action happened at a specific, finished time in the past.
For specific past times, we use the past simple tense.
'Broke' is the past simple form of 'break'.
hints: Which time expression tells us the action is finished?
For finished past actions, use the past simple.
'Break' is an irregular verb. What is its past simple form?
prompt: My cousin ______ his leg during the football match yesterday.

explanation: Good catch! The present continuous needs the '-ing' form of the verb.
explanationSteps: 'Right now' tells us the action is happening at this moment.
The present continuous uses 'am/is/are + verb-ing'.
So we need 'is watching', not 'is watch'.
hints: Which time expression tells us the action is happening now?
Present continuous structure: 'is + verb-ing'.
What is the '-ing' form of 'watch'?
prompt: My class is watch a documentary about marine life right now.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Will fly' sounds like a spontaneous decision, but visiting grandma is usually a fixed plan.
wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Going to fly' shows intention, but present continuous is better for diary arrangements.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present simple 'flies' is for habits and schedules, not a specific trip next month.

explanation: Perfect! When we have a definite future arrangement, the present continuous is the best choice.
explanationSteps: 'Next month' is a specific future time.
The present continuous can be used for fixed future plans and arrangements.
'Is flying' shows that the trip is already planned and arranged.
hints: Is visiting grandma a fixed plan or a sudden decision?
For definite future arrangements, which tense do we often use?
The family is plural, but here it acts as a unit with 'is'.
prompt: My family ______ to visit my grandma in Italy next month.

explanation: Excellent! The past continuous describes a background action that was interrupted.
explanationSteps: 'When the phone rang' is the interrupting action in the past.
The longer background action uses past continuous: 'was/were + verb-ing'.
Since 'my mum' is singular, we use 'was cooking'.
hints: Which action was the longer background activity?
Past continuous for background actions: 'was + verb-ing'.
'Mum' is singular, so use 'was' not 'were'.
prompt: My mum ______ ______ dinner when the phone rang. (cook)

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Past simple 'did you buy' needs a specific past time, but 'since' needs present perfect.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Present simple 'do you have' doesn't work with 'since', which refers to a period starting in the past.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Have you bought' focuses on the buying action, but the sentence is about possession over time.

explanation: Great job! 'Since' is a perfect partner for the present perfect tense.
explanationSteps: 'Since last summer' tells us the possession started in the past and continues to now.
The present perfect 'have you had' shows possession over a period of time.
'Have had' uses 'have' as a main verb meaning possession.
hints: Which time word pairs with present perfect to show duration?
'Since' needs the present perfect tense.
'Have' can mean possession, not just auxiliary.
prompt: How long ______ ______ this bicycle? I have had mine since last summer.

explanation: Not quite! The future perfect talks about completion before a future time, not just any future action.
explanationSteps: 'Will have finished' means completion before a specific future moment.
'By 6 PM' is the future time, and the homework must be done before then.
Simple future 'will finish' doesn't emphasize completion before another future event.
hints: Does 'will have finished' mean the same as 'will finish'?
Future perfect emphasizes completion before another future moment.
Think about the difference in meaning between the two sentences.
prompt: 'I will have finished my homework by 6 PM' means the same as 'I will finish my homework by 6 PM'.

explanation: Excellent! The past perfect continuous shows a longer action that happened before another past action.
explanationSteps: 'For an hour' tells us the running was a continuous past activity.
The past perfect continuous uses 'had been + verb-ing'.
It shows the running happened before and up to the moment of stopping.
hints: Which action happened first: the running or the stopping?
For longer actions before another past action, use 'had been + verb-ing'.
Double-check the past participle of 'run'.
prompt: She was exhausted because she ______ ______ ______ for an hour before she stopped. (run)

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Like' is a stative verb and is not normally used in continuous forms.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Doesn't like' is for third person singular, but the subject here is 'I'.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Am not like' means 'I am not similar to', which changes the meaning completely.

explanation: Perfect! 'Like' is a state verb, so we use the present simple, not the continuous.
explanationSteps: 'Like' describes a feeling or state, not an action in progress.
State verbs are usually used in simple tenses, not continuous forms.
For 'I', the negative present simple is 'don't like'.
hints: Is 'like' an action you can see happening, or a feeling?
For feelings and states, we usually use the present simple.
The subject is 'I', so use 'don't' not 'doesn't'.
prompt: I ______ ______ spicy food because it makes my stomach hurt.

originalSentence: At 8 PM tomorrow, I will watch the match.
answerKey: At 8 PM tomorrow, I will be watching the match.
acceptableAnswers: At 8 PM tomorrow, I will be watching the match.
explanation: Excellent transformation! The future continuous shows an action in progress at a specific future time.
explanationSteps: 'At 8 PM tomorrow' is a specific future moment.
The future continuous uses 'will be + verb-ing'.
It shows the match will be in progress at that exact time.
hints: Which structure shows an action in progress at a future moment?
Future continuous: 'will be + verb-ing'.
Keep the time expression at the beginning of the sentence.
prompt: Rewrite the sentence using the future continuous. Start with 'At 8 PM tomorrow...'

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Will turn' is for first conditional future results, but this is a general scientific truth.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Would turn' is for imaginary second conditional situations.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Turned' is past simple, but freezing water is a general fact, not a past event.

explanation: Great! The zero conditional is perfect for general truths and scientific facts.
explanationSteps: The zero conditional uses 'if + present simple, ... present simple' for general truths.
Water freezing at 0 degrees is a scientific fact.
Both clauses use the present simple tense.
hints: Is this a general scientific fact or a specific future possibility?
Zero conditional structure: if + present simple, ... present simple.
Think about what always happens when water freezes.
prompt: If you freeze water to 0 degrees Celsius, it ______ into ice.

explanation: Excellent! This first conditional shows a real possibility and its future result.
explanationSteps: 'If you finish your homework' is a real possibility.
First conditional uses 'if + present simple, ... will + base verb'.
The result is a real future consequence of completing the homework.
hints: Is finishing homework a real possibility?
First conditional: if + present simple, ... will + infinitive.
Use the base form of the verb in the if-clause.
prompt: If you ______ your homework early, we ______ go to the cinema tonight. (finish)

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Will buy' is first conditional, but having a million pounds is imaginary, not real.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Bought' is past simple, but we need 'would + infinitive' in the main clause of second conditional.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Would have bought' is third conditional for past regrets, not imaginary present situations.

explanation: Wonderful! You've used the second conditional to imagine an unreal present situation.
explanationSteps: Having a million pounds is an imaginary situation.
Second conditional uses 'if + past simple, ... would + infinitive'.
'Would buy' shows the imaginary result of having all that money.
hints: Is having a million pounds a real situation or imaginary?
Second conditional result: 'would + infinitive'.
The if-clause uses past simple 'had'.
prompt: If I had a million pounds, I ______ a house by the sea.

explanation: Great catch! The if-clause uses past perfect, so the main clause needs 'would have + past participle'.
explanationSteps: 'If I had studied' is the past perfect if-clause of a third conditional.
Third conditional main clause uses 'would have + past participle'.
So we need 'would have passed' to match the past unreal situation.
hints: Does the if-clause refer to the past or the present?
Third conditional: if + past perfect, ... would have + past participle.
Look at the verb form in the if-clause for clues.
prompt: If I had studied harder, I would pass the exam with top marks.

originalSentence: You might miss the bus if you don't leave now.
answerKey: You will miss the bus unless you leave now.
acceptableAnswers: You will miss the bus unless you leave now.
You might miss the bus unless you leave now.
explanation: Excellent transformation! 'Unless' means 'if not' and makes the sentence more concise.
explanationSteps: 'Unless' replaces 'if not' in conditional sentences.
'Unless you leave now' means 'if you don't leave now'.
The main clause keeps the future result.
hints: Which word means 'if not'?
Replace 'if you don't' with 'unless you'.
Keep the main clause result the same.
prompt: Rewrite this sentence using 'unless'. Keep the same meaning.

explanation: Perfect! You've created a beautiful second conditional sentence about an imaginary situation.
explanationSteps: Living in Paris is imaginary — it's not the current reality.
Second conditional: 'if + past simple, ... would + infinitive'.
'Would visit' shows the imaginary result of living there.
hints: Is living in Paris your current reality?
Use past simple in the if-clause and 'would + infinitive' in the main clause.
'Live' becomes 'lived' in the if-clause.
prompt: If I ______ in Paris, I ______ the Eiffel Tower every weekend. (live / visit)

explanation: Exactly right! The zero conditional is all about general truths and facts.
explanationSteps: Zero conditional uses 'if + present simple, ... present simple'.
It describes things that are always true, like scientific facts.
'If you heat ice, it melts' is a perfect example of a general truth.
hints: Does the zero conditional talk about specific future events?
Zero conditional is for general truths and scientific facts.
Both clauses use the same tense.
prompt: In zero conditional sentences, we use the present simple in both the if-clause and the main clause.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Would get' is for imaginary second conditional situations, but eating vegetables is a real health choice.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Gets' is present simple, but the main clause of first conditional needs 'will + infinitive'.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Would have got' is third conditional for past unreal situations, not future health results.

explanation: Well done! This first conditional connects a real health choice to its likely future result.
explanationSteps: Eating more vegetables is a real possibility.
First conditional: 'if + present simple, ... will + infinitive'.
'Will get' shows the likely future health benefit.
hints: Is eating more vegetables a real possibility?
First conditional result uses 'will + infinitive'.
Think about the structure: if + present simple, will + base verb.
prompt: If you eat more vegetables, you ______ stronger and healthier.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Makes' is active voice, but paper doesn't make itself — people make it from wood.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Made' is past simple active or past participle, but we need a present passive verb here.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Is making' is present continuous active, which would mean the paper is doing the making.

explanation: Perfect! The paper receives the action, so we need the present passive voice.
explanationSteps: The subject 'paper' receives the action — it is made by people.
Present passive uses 'am/is/are + past participle'.
Since 'paper' is singular, we use 'is made'.
hints: Does paper make itself, or is it made by someone?
Present passive for singular subjects: 'is + past participle'.
What is the past participle of 'make'?
prompt: Paper ______ from wood pulp in factories all over the world.

explanation: Excellent! The telephone received the action of invention in the past.
explanationSteps: The telephone didn't invent itself — Alexander Graham Bell did.
'In 1876' tells us this happened in the past.
Past passive for singular subjects: 'was + past participle'.
hints: Did the telephone invent itself?
Past passive: 'was + past participle' for singular subjects.
What is the past participle of 'invent'?
prompt: The telephone ______ ______ by Alexander Graham Bell in 1876. (invent)

originalSentence: A famous architect designed this bridge.
answerKey: This bridge was designed by a famous architect.
acceptableAnswers: This bridge was designed by a famous architect.
explanation: Wonderful! You shifted the focus to the bridge and included the agent for extra information.
explanationSteps: The object 'this bridge' becomes the new subject.
Past passive: 'was + past participle' because the bridge is singular.
We included 'by a famous architect' to mention who designed it.
hints: Make 'this bridge' the new subject.
Use 'was + past participle' and include 'by a famous architect'.
Keep the same tense: past simple passive.
prompt: Change this sentence to the passive voice. Begin with 'This bridge...'

explanation: Great catch! Passive voice needs the past participle, not the base form.
explanationSteps: 'Are build' mixes the auxiliary verb with the base form.
Present passive uses 'are + past participle'.
'Built' is the past participle of 'build'.
hints: Is 'build' the past participle of 'build'?
Passive voice needs 'are + past participle'.
What is the past participle of 'build'?
prompt: These houses are build with recycled materials to help the environment.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Will complete' is active voice, but the stadium doesn't complete itself — workers do.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Is completing' is present continuous active, which would mean the stadium is doing the completing.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Completes' is present simple active and doesn't fit the future time 'next year'.

explanation: Perfect! The stadium will receive the action, so future passive is exactly right.
explanationSteps: The stadium is the receiver of the action, not the doer.
'Next year' tells us we're talking about the future.
Future passive: 'will be + past participle'.
hints: Will the stadium complete itself?
Future passive structure: 'will be + past participle'.
What is the past participle of 'complete'?
prompt: The new stadium ______ ______ by next year. (complete)

explanation: Excellent! When we combine a modal with passive voice, we use 'modal + be + past participle'.
explanationSteps: 'Must' is a modal verb expressing obligation.
Modal passive structure: 'modal + be + past participle'.
'Must be worn' shows that helmets are required by rules.
hints: Which modal shows strong obligation?
Modal passive: 'must + be + past participle'.
What is the past participle of 'wear'?
prompt: Helmets ______ ______ ______ by all cyclists on this road. (must / wear)

explanation: Not necessarily! We can form passive sentences in any tense, not just the past.
explanationSteps: Passive voice exists in all tenses: present, past, future, perfect, and continuous.
Examples: 'is eaten' (present), 'was eaten' (past), 'will be eaten' (future).
Any tense can be turned into passive voice!
hints: Can you think of a present passive sentence?
Passive voice works in every tense.
Try making a passive sentence in the present tense.
prompt: Passive voice can only be used in the past tense.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Speaks' is active voice, but English doesn't speak itself — people speak it.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Was spoken' is past passive, but the sentence is a general fact about the present.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Has spoken' is present perfect active and doesn't fit this general statement.

explanation: Perfect! English is the object of speaking, so present passive is the natural choice.
explanationSteps: English is spoken by people, so it receives the action.
This is a general fact, so we use the present passive.
'Is spoken' shows it's a current, ongoing situation.
hints: Does English speak itself?
For general facts about actions received, use present passive.
What is the past participle of 'speak'?
prompt: English ______ by over a billion people around the world.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Are' is plural, but 'the audience' acts as a single unit when reacting together.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Were' is past tense, but 'now' tells us this is happening in the present.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Have been' is present perfect and doesn't fit the immediate reaction described.

explanation: Great! When a collective noun acts as one unit, a singular verb is the best choice.
explanationSteps: 'The audience' is a collective noun that can act as a single unit.
When the group reacts together, use a singular verb.
'Is clapping' shows the audience acting as one.
hints: Is the audience acting as one group or as separate individuals?
Collective nouns often take singular verbs when the group acts together.
'Now' tells us this is happening in the present.
prompt: The audience ______ clapping loudly after the school play.

explanation: Excellent! You looked past the prepositional phrase to find the true subject.
explanationSteps: Remove 'of old books' to find the real subject.
The subject is 'the collection', which is singular.
So the verb must be singular: 'is'.
hints: Cross out 'of old books' to find the real subject.
Is the true subject singular or plural?
The collection is one thing, even though it contains many books.
prompt: The collection of old books in the library are very valuable.

explanation: Perfect! With 'either...or', the verb agrees with the noun closest to it.
explanationSteps: In 'either...or' constructions, look at the noun nearest to the verb.
Here, 'the teacher' is singular and closest to the verb.
So we use the singular verb 'is'.
hints: With 'either...or', which noun is closer to the verb?
The verb agrees with the nearest subject.
'The teacher' is singular, so use 'is'.
prompt: Either the students or the teacher ______ going to present the project tomorrow.

explanation: Not quite! 'Neither' is singular and takes a singular verb.
explanationSteps: 'Neither of the options' refers to 'not one and not the other'.
When 'neither' is the subject, it takes a singular verb.
The correct form is 'Neither of the options is correct.'
hints: Does 'neither' mean one thing or two things?
'Neither' is singular and takes a singular verb.
Think of 'neither' as 'not either one'.
prompt: The sentence 'Neither of the options are correct' is grammatically correct.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Is' is singular, but the real subject after 'there' is 'three teams', which is plural.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Were' is past tense, but 'this year' suggests a present time frame.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Has been' is singular and doesn't match the plural subject 'three teams'.

explanation: Perfect! In 'there be' sentences, the verb agrees with the real subject that follows.
explanationSteps: In 'there is/are' sentences, find the subject after the verb.
The real subject is 'three teams', which is plural.
So we use the plural verb 'are'.
hints: Which noun comes after 'there' in this sentence?
The verb agrees with the real subject, not with 'there'.
'Three teams' is plural, so use 'are'.
prompt: There ______ three teams competing in the tournament this year.

explanation: Excellent! 'Each' is always singular and takes a singular verb.
explanationSteps: 'Each student' means every single student individually.
'Each' is always singular, even when followed by a plural noun.
So we use the singular verb 'has'.
hints: Does 'each' refer to one person or many people?
'Each' is always singular and takes a singular verb.
Think about 'each one' to help you remember.
prompt: Each student in my class ______ a unique talent to share.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'At' is used for specific times like 'at 5 o'clock', not for days of the week.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'In' is used for months, seasons, and years, not specific days.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'By Monday' means before or no later than Monday, not during Monday.

explanation: Perfect! Days of the week always pair with the preposition 'on'.
explanationSteps: We use 'on' for specific days and dates.
'Monday' is a day of the week.
So 'on Monday' is the correct choice.
hints: Which preposition do we use with days of the week?
Think: on days, in months, at times.
Monday is a specific day.
prompt: We have a science test ______ Monday morning.

explanation: Excellent! You've matched each noun with its correct preposition partner.
explanationSteps: We use 'on' for surfaces like a table or desk.
We use 'in' for containers like a drawer.
'On the desk' and 'in the drawer' are standard preposition combinations.
hints: Which preposition do we use for a surface?
Which preposition do we use for inside a container?
A desk is a surface, and a drawer is inside a desk.
prompt: Put the book ______ the desk and the pencils ______ the drawer.

explanation: Great catch! 'Afraid' always pairs with 'of' in English.
explanationSteps: The adjective 'afraid' pairs with 'of' to show what causes fear.
'Afraid from' is not standard English.
'Afraid of' is the correct collocation.
hints: Which preposition follows 'afraid'?
'Afraid of' is a fixed collocation in English.
Think about common phrases like 'afraid of the dark'.
prompt: My sister is afraid from spiders, even the tiny ones.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Through' means going inside something, like a tunnel or forest, not over a flat surface.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Between' is used for two things, like between two buildings, not a bridge.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Along' means following a line, like a road or river, not crossing over something.

explanation: Perfect! 'Across' is the preposition we use when moving from one side to the other of a surface.
explanationSteps: A bridge is a surface that connects two sides of a river.
'Across' means from one side to the other.
So 'walked across the bridge' is the natural choice.
hints: Does the bridge connect two sides?
'Across' means from one side to another.
Think about crossing a road or a field.
prompt: We walked ______ the bridge to get to the other side of the river.

explanation: Exactly right! 'Look after' is a phrasal verb that means to take care of someone or something.
explanationSteps: 'Look after' means to take care of or be responsible for.
It is a phrasal verb where the preposition 'after' changes the meaning of 'look'.
Other examples: look for, look up, look forward to.
hints: Does 'look after' mean the same as 'take care of'?
Phrasal verbs combine a verb and a preposition with a special meaning.
Think about babysitting or pet sitting.
prompt: The phrasal verb 'look after' means to take care of someone or something.

explanation: Excellent! You've used the correct prepositions for time and date.
explanationSteps: We use 'at' for specific times like 'at 9 o'clock'.
We use 'on' for specific days and dates like 'on Saturday'.
'At 9 o'clock on Saturday' follows the standard rules.
hints: Which preposition do we use with a specific time?
Which preposition do we use with a day of the week?
Remember: at times, on days, in months.
prompt: The train leaves ______ 9 o'clock ______ Saturday morning.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Must' means obligation, but the sentence is asking for permission, not giving a rule.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Should' gives advice, but here we need to ask if something is allowed.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Might' shows possibility, not permission.

explanation: Perfect! 'Can' is the friendly modal we use to ask for permission.
explanationSteps: The sentence is a polite request for permission.
'Can' is commonly used to ask if something is allowed.
'Can I borrow your pen?' is a natural way to ask.
hints: Are we asking for permission or giving advice?
'Can' is a common modal for asking permission.
Think about what you say when you want to borrow something.
prompt: ______ I borrow your pen for a moment, please?

explanation: Excellent! 'Could' is the past tense of 'can' and shows past ability.
explanationSteps: 'When I was five' tells us we are talking about the past.
'Could' is the past form of 'can' and shows ability in the past.
'Could swim' means I had the ability to swim at that age.
hints: Does the sentence talk about the past or the present?
What is the past tense of 'can'?
'Could' shows past ability.
prompt: When I was five, I ______ ______ across the pool without any help.

explanation: Great catch! After 'must not', we use the base form of the verb without 'to'.
explanationSteps: 'Must not' is a modal verb phrase.
Modal verbs are followed directly by the base verb.
So we say 'must not touch', not 'must not to touch'.
hints: Do modal verbs need 'to' before the next verb?
After 'must not', use the base form directly.
'Must not' means prohibition — it is not allowed.
prompt: You must not to touch the paintings in the museum.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'Might' shows possibility, but the lights being on is strong evidence that someone is home.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Can' shows ability, not logical deduction from evidence.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Should' gives advice or expectation, but we are making a deduction, not giving advice.

explanation: Brilliant deduction! 'Must' is the modal we use when we are very certain based on evidence.
explanationSteps: The lights being on is strong evidence.
'Must' shows we are almost certain based on what we see.
It means we are making a logical conclusion.
hints: How certain are we based on the evidence?
For strong logical deductions, use 'must'.
Think about what the lights being on tells us.
prompt: The lights are on, so someone ______ be at home.

originalSentence: It was necessary for me to wear a uniform at my old school.
answerKey: I had to wear a uniform at my old school.
acceptableAnswers: I had to wear a uniform at my old school.
explanation: Excellent transformation! 'Had to' is the past form of 'must' and shows past obligation.
explanationSteps: 'Had to' replaces 'it was necessary' in the past.
It shows that wearing a uniform was an obligation in the past.
'Had to' is the past tense of 'must' for obligation.
hints: Which modal phrase shows past obligation?
'Had to' means it was necessary in the past.
Make 'I' the subject of the new sentence.
prompt: Rewrite this sentence using 'had to'. Keep the same meaning about past obligation.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'Who' is for people, not things like books.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: 'Where' is for places, not objects.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: 'Whose' shows possession, but we don't need possession here.

explanation: Perfect! 'Which' is the relative pronoun we use for things in defining clauses.
explanationSteps: The antecedent is 'the book', which is a thing.
For things in defining relative clauses, we use 'which' or 'that'.
'Which was written last year' defines which book we mean.
hints: Are we talking about a person or a thing?
For things, 'which' is a great choice.
The clause tells us which specific book.
prompt: This is the book ______ was written by my favourite author last year.

explanation: Excellent! For things in non-defining clauses, 'which' is the correct choice.
explanationSteps: The clause gives extra information about Mount Everest.
Non-defining clauses use commas and 'which' for things.
'Which' is preferred over 'that' in non-defining clauses.
hints: Is this clause essential or just extra information?
Non-defining clauses use 'which' for things.
Notice the commas around the clause.
prompt: Mount Everest, ______ is the highest mountain in the world, attracts many climbers.

explanation: Great catch! For people, we use 'who' or 'that', not 'which'.
explanationSteps: The antecedent is 'the girl', which is a person.
For people in relative clauses, 'who' is the natural choice.
'Which' is reserved for things and animals.
hints: Is a girl a person or a thing?
For people, use 'who' or 'that'.
'Which' is for things, not people.
prompt: The girl which sits next to me in class is really good at maths.

explanation: Exactly right! In defining relative clauses, we can often omit the relative pronoun when it is the object.
explanationSteps: When the relative pronoun is the object of the clause, we can leave it out.
'The book (that) I read' — 'that' is the object, so it can be omitted.
This is only possible in defining clauses, not non-defining clauses.
hints: Is the relative pronoun the subject or the object of the clause?
Object relative pronouns can often be omitted.
Try saying the sentence without the relative pronoun.
prompt: In defining relative clauses, we can sometimes omit the relative pronoun when it is the object of the clause.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: 'A' is indefinite and used for general singular nouns, but we are talking about a specific sun.
wrongChoiceReasons.B: 'An' is also indefinite and used before vowel sounds, but we need the definite article here.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: We need an article because 'sun' is a singular countable noun and we are referring to the specific sun in our solar system.

explanation: Perfect! We use 'the' when there is only one of something that everyone knows about.
explanationSteps: There is only one sun in our solar system.
We use 'the' for unique things that everyone knows.
'The sun' is a specific, unique object.
hints: Is there more than one sun in our solar system?
We use 'the' for unique things.
Think about 'the moon' and 'the earth' too.
prompt: ______ sun rises in the east and sets in the west.

explanation: Excellent! We use 'a' before singular countable nouns that start with a consonant sound.
explanationSteps: 'Uniform' starts with a consonant sound 'yoo'.
Even though 'uniform' starts with the letter 'u', the sound is what matters.
So we use 'a' instead of 'an'.
hints: What sound does 'uniform' start with?
It is the sound, not the letter, that matters.
'Uniform' starts with a 'y' sound.
prompt: My school requires everyone to wear ______ uniform on special occasions.

explanation: Not quite! 'Little' and 'a little' have different meanings.
explanationSteps: 'A little' means some, a small amount — it is positive.
'Little' means almost none — it is negative.
'I have a little time' means I have some time. 'I have little time' means I almost have no time.
hints: Does 'a little' mean the same as 'little'?
'A little' is positive, but 'little' is negative.
Think about the difference between 'a few friends' and 'few friends'.
prompt: 'I have a little time' and 'I have little time' mean exactly the same thing.
